era_context: The early 15th century spans the late medieval period in Europe, the Ming Dynasty's consolidation in China, the Ottoman Empire's expansion in the Balkans and Anatolia, and the Zheng He treasure voyages in the Indian Ocean. In Europe, the Great Schism in the Catholic Church is being addressed at the Council of Pisa; the Hundred Years' War continues between England and France; and the Portuguese Age of Exploration is beginning. In Africa, the Mali Empire has fragmented, and the Songhai Empire is rising. In the Americas, the Aztec Empire under Montezuma I is expanding, and the Inca Empire is consolidating power under Pachacuti's successors.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited European documentation; oral traditions and later compilations dominate)
Southeast Asia and the Pacific (sparse European records; Chinese sources exist but may be underrepresented)
Indigenous Americas (reliance on post-conquest Spanish accounts and archaeological inference)

events.title: Council of Pisa convenes to resolve the Great Schism
events.description: The Council of Pisa opens on 25 March 1409, convened to end the Western Schism in the Catholic Church, which had divided papal allegiance between Rome and Avignon since 1378. Bishops and cardinals from across Christendom gather to address the crisis of competing papal claimants. The council deposes both Pope Gregory XII (Rome) and Antipope Benedict XIII (Avignon) and elects Alexander V as a compromise candidate. However, the two deposed claimants refuse to acknowledge the council's authority, temporarily creating three papal claimants instead of two, deepening the crisis before eventual resolution at Constance (1414–1418).
key_figures: Cardinal Baldassare Cossa (later Pope John XXIII)

sources.name: Dietrich of Niem, De Schismate (contemporary chronicle)
Historiographic consensus on the Council of Pisa

events.certainty_note: The Council of Pisa is well-documented in contemporary chronicles and official council records. The date of 25 March 1409 is firmly established.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure fleet departs on its second voyage
events.description: Admiral Zheng He (鄭和), commanding a massive treasure fleet of over 300 ships and 27,000 men, departs from Nanjing in late 1409 on the second of his seven great voyages. The fleet carries diplomatic gifts, trade goods, and military personnel, sailing to Southeast Asia, the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and the Arabian Peninsula. These voyages represent the peak of Ming naval power and exploration, establishing tributary relationships and demonstrating Chinese maritime supremacy. The voyages cease after 1433, following a shift in imperial policy toward land-based defense and Confucian orthodoxy's skepticism of maritime commerce.

sources.name: Luo Maodeng, Longjiang Baochuan (later compilation, 16th century)
Archaeological evidence from shipwrecks and port sites

events.certainty_note: Zheng He's voyages are well-documented in Ming imperial records. The second voyage's departure date is established as late 1409 or early 1410 in contemporary sources.
cross_references: Part of broader Ming maritime expansion (1405–1433)

events.title: Battle of Agincourt preparations and English military mobilization
events.description: Following the assassination of Louis, Duke of Orléans in 1407, factional conflict between Burgundians and Armagnacs intensifies in France. In England, King Henry V consolidates power and begins military preparations for a renewed invasion of France, claiming the French throne. The English mobilize armies, secure alliances with Burgundy, and prepare for the campaign that will culminate in the Battle of Agincourt in October 1415. These preparations represent a major escalation in the Hundred Years' War and signal Henry V's aggressive foreign policy.

sources.name: Froissart, Chronicles (near-contemporary, compiled earlier but updated)
English royal records and parliamentary rolls
Historiographic consensus on Henry V's reign

events.certainty_note: The general timeline of Henry V's military preparations is well-established. Specific events in 1409 are inferred from broader political context rather than single-year documentation.
cross_references: Follows from 1407-001 (assassination of Louis of Orléans)

events.title: Portuguese exploration of the African coast accelerates
events.description: Under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator, Portuguese expeditions continue to explore and map the West African coast. By 1409, Portuguese ships have established trading posts and forts along the Moroccan and Mauritanian coasts, seeking gold, spices, and enslaved people. These early voyages lay the groundwork for the Portuguese Age of Exploration and the eventual rounding of the Cape of Good Hope. The expeditions are motivated by economic gain, religious conversion, and geographic curiosity, and they mark the beginning of European colonial expansion in Africa.

sources.name: Gomes Eanes de Zurara, Chronicle of the Discovery and Conquest of Guinea (15th-century compilation)
Portuguese royal records and trading post documentation
Archaeological evidence from Portuguese forts in West Africa

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of the African coast is well-documented, but specific events in 1409 are less precisely dated. The general trajectory of exploration is confirmed; individual voyage dates may be approximate.
cross_references: Part of broader Portuguese Age of Exploration (1415–1500s)

disconfirming_evidence: The Council of Pisa is sometimes portrayed as a decisive resolution to the Great Schism, but modern scholarship emphasizes that it actually worsened the crisis by creating three papal claimants instead of two. The council's authority was disputed, and its elected pope (Alexander V) was not universally recognized. The schism was not truly resolved until the Council of Constance (1414–1418). Additionally, the significance of Zheng He's voyages has been subject to historiographic debate: some scholars argue they were primarily diplomatic and tributary missions rather than voyages of discovery or trade expansion, and their cessation after 1433 reflects deliberate imperial policy rather than decline. The 'Age of Exploration' framing applied to Portuguese activities in 1409 is somewhat anachronistic; these were primarily trading and raiding expeditions, not systematic exploration in the later sense.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1409 is uneven. European events (Council of Pisa, English-French conflict) are well-recorded in contemporary chronicles and official records, with a bias toward Western institutional history. Chinese sources (Ming Shilu) provide detailed records of Zheng He's voyages, though these reflect imperial court perspectives. African and indigenous American history relies heavily on later European accounts, oral traditions, and archaeology, introducing significant gaps and potential biases. The 'Age of Exploration' narrative itself reflects a Eurocentric historiographic tradition that privileges European agency and discovery over non-European maritime activity (such as Zheng He's voyages, which were arguably more extensive but are often marginalized in Western historical accounts).

graph_edges.note: Council of Pisa's failure to resolve the schism led directly to the convening of the Council of Constance
Second voyage is part of Zheng He's broader series of treasure voyages (1405–1433)
English military preparations in 1409 culminated in the Battle of Agincourt in 1415
Portuguese exploration in 1409 is part of the broader Age of Exploration (15th–17th centuries)
